pages.text: Workability, and mechanical, acoustic and thermal properties of lightweight aggregate concrete with a high volume of entrained air H.K. Kim, J.H. Jeon, H.K. Lee ⇑ Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology, Guseong-dong, Yuseong-gu, Daejeon 305-70 1, South Korea article info Article history: Received 23 March 2011 Received in revised form 18 August 2011 Accepted 18 August 2011 Available online 24 November 2011 Keywords: Lightweight aggregates Foamed concrete Expanded shale Bottom ash Air entraining agent Thermal conductivity Acoustic transmission loss abstract This study investigated the various characteristics of lightweight aggregate concrete with a high volume of entrained air. The effects of lightweight aggregates and entrained air on the ﬂow characteristics, den- sity, porosity, compressive strength, and dynamic elastic modulus of the concrete were investigated. In addition, the acoustic transmission loss and thermal conductivity – essential performance measures for architectural construction materials – of lightweight aggregate concrete with a high volume of entrained air were measured. The lightweight aggregate cellular concrete containing an adequate amount of air entraining agent was shown to have excellent characteristics including very-high workability, low density and proper strength, and it can be applied in architectural members with high acoustic shielding and thermal insulating properties. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Lightweight concrete has been preferred over normal concrete due to a number of improved properties. The most prevailing ben- eﬁt of lightweight concrete is decreased structural dead weight [11]. The decrease of dead weight could lead to reduced construc- tion costs, since it can decrease the size of the foundation and structural members such as columns and thickness of walls [21]. Furthermore, lightweight concrete has lower thermal conductivity than normal concrete due to higher porosity inside the material [5]. Since the internal structure is not dense, lightweight concrete structural members are deformed and destroyed easily. Hence, after destruction, crushed materials can also be remanufactured easily as part of recycling processes. Therefore, lightweight con- crete has a low lifecycle cost [21]. Lightweight concrete is generally classiﬁed into two types: aer- ated concrete (or foamed concrete) and lightweight aggregate con- crete [21]. The aerated concrete has very light weight and low thermal conductivity [21]. However, an autoclaving process is essential to obtain a certain level of strength, which requires a spe- cial manufacturing plant and consumes very high energy. In con- trast, lightweight aggregate concrete, which is manufactured without an autoclaving process, has higher strength but shows high- er density and lower thermal conductivity than aerated concrete. By introducing entrained air into lightweight aggregate con- crete, the density and the thermal conductivity of the concrete can be improved [30,17]. The product of this concept is referred to as lightweight aggregate cellular concrete (LACC, or lightweight aggregate foamed concrete) [30,17]. This material has higher strength than aerated concrete and lower density and lower ther- mal insulation than conventional lightweight aggregate concrete [30]. Moreover, since LACC does not need an autoclave process, construction costs are decreased. It also h

compressive strength, and dynamic elastic modulus of the concrete were investigated. In addition, the acoustic transmission loss and thermal conductivity – essential performance measures for archi- tectural construction materials – of lightweight aggregate concrete with a high volume of entrained air were measured. 2. Experiments 2.1. Materials Type I Portland cement was used as a binder material. The details of the char- acteristics of the cement used are listed in Table 1. For reference specimens (normal concrete), river sand and crashed gravel were used as ﬁne and coarse aggregates, respectively. Meanwhile, ﬁne bottom ash and coarse expanded shale were used as ﬁne and coarse aggregates, respectively, for lightweight aggregate concrete spec- imens. Fine bottom ash was obtained from by-products of the pulverized coal ﬁring boiler system at Seocheon thermal power plant. The combustion temperature of the power plant was within 1400–1550 /C176C. The particle size of river sand and ﬁne bot- tom ash, respectively, was controlled within a range of 0.125–0.600 mm by sieving prior to mixing. The coarse expanded shale used in this study was manufactured with a high sintering temperature of 1150–1200 /C176C. Maximum size of crushed grav- el and expanded shale was 19 mm. The physical properties of the aggregates are listed in Table 2 . In addition, pore volume of the ﬁne bottom ash and coarse ex- panded shale was measured by the Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) method for small size pores with a radius between 1.7 nm and 300 nm, as shown in Fig. 1 . The porosity of the aggregates is deﬁned as the volume ratio of the pores and craters in the aggregates and is given by (c.f. ASTM C 127 and C 128) Porosityðvol:%Þ¼ Vpores Vaggregates /C2 100 ¼ W2 /C0 W3 W2 /C0 W1 /C2 100 ð1Þ where Vpores and Vaggregates are the volume of the pores and the aggregates, respec- tively, W1 is the weight of the aggregates under water, W2 is the weight of the aggre- gates with a surface saturated dry (S.S.D.) condition after 2-week soaking and boiling in a water tank at a temperature of 99 ± 3 /C176C for 1 day to eliminate the residuary air, and W3 is the weight of the aggregates after being completely dried in an oven (at more than 110 /C176C) for 24 h. To generate air bubbles in the concrete mixture, an AE agent that satisﬁed the ASTM C 260 speciﬁcations was used. The details of the air entraining agent are listed in Table 3 . 2.2. Mix proportion The mix proportion used in this study is listed in Table 4. The concrete with nor- mal aggregates was designed as a reference along with the lightweight aggregates concrete. The water–cement ratio ( w/c) and the dosage of cement of both type of concrete were ﬁxed at 0.47 and 365 kg/m 3, respectively. For lightweight aggregate concrete, 100% ﬁne bottom ash and coarse expanded shale were used. The sand– aggregate ratio ( S/a) of normal and lightweight aggregate concrete were 0.48 and 0.32, respectively. The air entraining agent was used for lightweight aggregate con- crete with four different weight percentages (0%, 0.5%, 1.0%, and 1.5%) of cement. 2.3. Experimental methods All batches were mixed in a 10 L capacity fan mixer. Fine and coarse aggregates were controlled in an S.S.D. condition prior to mixing. Cement and ﬁne aggregate were mixed for 1 min. and water with the air entraining agent were added and then mixed for 5 min. more. Finally, coarse aggregates were added and the cement mix- ture was mixed again for 2 min. The slump ﬂ

aggregates were used in a pre-wetted condition and consequently water content in the cement paste was not changed by water absorption of the aggregates. This phenomenon can be explained by the following process. With an increase of addition of AE agent, the volume of pores in fresh concrete increased and the thickness of the cement paste ﬁlm surrounding the pores decreased. In this case, the stability of the pores decreased [22,25]. Some parts of the cement paste ﬁlm col- lapsed and the size of the pores increased due to coalescence of pores [6]. Although coalescence of pores occurred, the size and dis- tribution of pores and the distribution of aggregate on the bottom surface were equivalent to these parameters on the top surface, as shown in Figs. 5 and 6 . It appears that ﬂoating of pores to the top surface was hindered by the lower ﬂuidity of the mortar containing ﬁne bottom ash with a rough shape. 3.3. Density and porosity Figs. 7 and 8 show the density and porosity of normal and light- weight concrete, respectively. In an S.S.D. condition, whereas the density of normal concrete was 2355 kg/m 3, the density of light- weight concrete was 1800 kg/m 3 when AE agent was not used and decreased to 1553 kg/m 3 with decreased addition of AE agent to 1.0%. However, the density increased to 1659 kg/m 3 when 1.5% of AE agent was used. Meanwhile, in the O.D. condition, the density of lightweight concrete was 1213 kg/m 3 when the addition of AE agent was 1.0%, but increased to 1331 kg/m 3 with the use of 1.5% of AE agent. The porosity of lightweight aggregates was increased from 29% to 34% when the addition of AE agent was increased from 0% to 1.0%, but a 33% decrease was observed when 1.5% of AE agent was used. It is believed that the porosity of concrete with the overuse of AE agent decreased due to the collapse of pores in the concrete. Be- cause the porosity and the pore size of fresh concrete increased with increased addition of AE agent, the thickness of the cement paste ﬁlm surrounding the pores lessened, and as a result, the pores easily collapsed before the cement paste hardened [25]. Therefore, the porosity was reduced and the density thereupon in- creased when excessive AE agent was employed. 3.4. Compressive strength and dynamic modulus of elasticity Figs. 9 and 10 show the compressive strength and dynamic modulus of elasticity, respectively. The compressive strength and the dynamic modulus of elasticity of normal concrete were 40 MPa and 30 GPa, respectively, and these values decreased to 22 MPa and 20 GPa, respectively, with replacement of lightweight aggregates. In addition, the compressive strength and dynamic modulus of elasticity of lightweight concrete both decreased to 8 MPa and 7 GPa, respectively, when the addition of AE agent was increased to 1.0%, and slightly increased further to 11 MPa and 9 GPa, respectively, with 1.5% of added AE agent. The effects of porosity on the compressive strength and dy- namic modulus of elasticity in this study are presented in Figs. 11 and 12. In general, the relation between porosity and compressive strength of ceramic materials and rocks has been calculated by regression analyzes using experimental results and a number of equations have been proposed, including the following [1,8,12,26–28]: rc ¼ r0ð1 /C0 pÞn ðBalshinÞð 4Þ rc ¼ r0e/C0 krp ðRyshkevitchÞð 5Þ rc ¼ k ln p0 p ðSchillerÞð 6Þ rc ¼ r0 /C0 kp ðHasselmannÞð 7Þ where rc and p are the compressive strength and porosity, respec- tively,

where ksm is the thermal conductivity of the solid matrix, kair is the thermal conductivity of air, p is the porosity in the material, and sdry is the tortuosity factor of a material in an O.D. condition [2,16].A detailed description of this conceptual model has been presented in a previous paper [2,16]. In accordance with the experimental re- sults reported by Laurent [16] and Bouguerra et al. [2], it was shown that the value of the tortuosity factor slightly increased or remained constant with an increase in porosity, when the pores were uni- formly distributed in a solid matrix. Considering the much lower thermal conductivity of air (0.02–0.03 W/m K) relative to that of ceramic materials (1–50 W/m K) [29], Eq. (8) denotes that the ther- mal conductivity of materials is high when the porosity in the ma- trix is high. In addition, as shown in Fig. 14 , the thermal conductivity line- arly decreased as the porosity increased, regardless of the location of the pores, which affects the compressive strength and dynamic modulus of elasticity. It was inferred that the thermal conductivity was not sensitively affected by the materials consisting of texture around the pores. 3.6. Acoustic transmission loss In general, the acoustic transmission loss (TL) of a thin single plate is affected by the input frequency range and the mass of material, as given by [7,9,24] TL ¼ 2 logðf /C1 mÞ/C0 48 ð9Þ where f is the frequency and m is the mass per unit area (kg/m 2). According to the given equation, a 50% reduction of mass of material results in a 6 dB decrease of the acoustic transmission loss [9]. However, this value might be ignored when the mass per unit area is high enough that the absolute value of the acoustic trans- mission loss is much higher than this. In addition, due to the high thickness of the concrete specimens, the acoustic energy absorp- tion must be considered, and Eq. (9) does not take into account the effect of acoustic energy absorption. Fig. 15 plots the experimental results of the acoustic transmis- sion loss of normal and lightweight concrete specimens measured by the impedance tube method. It is seen that the acoustic trans- mission loss of normal concrete was nearly constant relative to that of lightweight concrete with and without AE agent. Moreover, regardless of the type of concrete specimen, the frequency of the coincidence effect, that is, the effect that the acoustic transmission loss suddenly decreased in a certain frequency range due to the material resonance, was uniformly in a range of 300–600 Hz. Therefore, it is considered that the acoustic shielding property of the concrete did not decline with a decrease of its density. 4. Concluding remarks The present paper has presented results of the investigation on the workability, and mechanical, acoustic, and thermal properties of lightweight aggregate concrete with a high volume of entrained air. The effects of lightweight aggregates and entrained air on the ﬂow characteristics, density, porosity, compressive strength, and dynamic elastic modulus of the concrete were investigated. In addition, the acoustic transmission loss and thermal conductivity of lightweight aggregate concrete with a high volume of entrained air were measured, as these are essential performance measures for architectural construction materials. The following conclusions have been drawn from the investigation. (i) Slump ﬂow of lightweight concrete drastically increased with the increase of AE agent fro

further with 1.5% of added AE agent. Moreover, the decrease of compressive strength was less sensitive when the pores were located in the lightweight aggregate, compared to the case where the pores form in the cement paste matrix. It is attrib- uted to the higher strength of materials consisting of texture in lightweight aggregates relative to that of cement paste. (iii) The thermal conductivity of concrete was decreased when normal aggregates were replaced by lightweight aggregates, and further decreased with addition of AE agent. Moreover, the thermal conductivity linearly decreased as the porosity increased, regardless of the location of the pores, which affects the strength and modulus of elasticity. However, the acoustic transmission loss of normal concrete was nearly constant relative to that of lightweight concrete with and without AE agent. In summary, the lightweight aggregate cellular concrete con- taining 0.5–1.0% of AE agent was shown to have excellent charac- teristics including very-high workability, low density and proper strength, and it can be applied in architectural members with high acoustic shielding and thermal insulating properties. The results of present study can be possibly used to establish guidelines for prac- tical applications of lightweight aggregate concrete foamed by AE agent, which has slightly different characteristics compared to both traditional concrete and conventional aerated concrete foamed by AE agent. Further research will be conducted along the line of this research in the future to accelerate the introduction of lightweight aggregate cellular concrete foamed by AE agent to civil infrastructures. Such research may include (1) the study on the effect of the curing temperature and condition on the strength development of lightweight aggregate cellular concrete foamed by AE agent, and (2) the investigation on the early and later age shrinkage of lightweight aggregate cellular concrete foamed by AE agent. Acknowledgments This research was sponsored by the IT R&D program of MKE/ IITA (Development of new IT convergence technology for smart building to improve the environment of electromagnetic waves, sound, and building). [8] Hasselmann DPH. On th

stress concentration was higher than that of the texture around the pores in the cement paste. Therefore, despite having the same porosity, the concrete might have higher compressive strength when the pores are concentrated in lightweight aggregates as com- pared to concrete with pores located in the cement paste matrix. 3.5. Thermal conductivity Fig. 13 shows that the thermal conductivity of lightweight con- crete is lower than that of normal concrete. While the thermal con- ductivity of normal concrete was 0.7–0.8 W/m K in a temperature range of 0–50 /C176C, that of lightweight concrete decreased to 0.5– 0.6 W/m K (63–70%) when AE agent was not used, and decreased further to 0.3–0.4 W/m K (52–55%) with the addition of AE agent up to 1.5%. The thermal conductivity has a strong relationship with the denseness of the matrix in materials [2,4,14] and is deﬁned as [2,16] kdry ¼ ð1 /C0 pÞksm þ pkair sdry ð8Þ Density [kg/m3] Addtion of AE agent [wt.%/cement] Normal conc. The thermal conductivity of 300 /C2 300 /C2 50 mm thick plate specimens was measured using a heat ﬂow meter (HFM436, manufactured by NETZSCH-Gerätebau GmbH) at temperatures of 0, 20, 30, and 50 /C176C, according to ASTM C 518. The exper- imental setup for measurement of thermal conductivity in this study is shown in Fig. 2 . Furthermore, the acoustic transmission loss of 45 /C2 90 mm cylinder speci- mens was measured using an impedance tube in a frequency range from 10 to 2000 Hz, as shown in Fig. 3 , following the ASTM E 2611 speciﬁcations. 3. Results and discussions 3.1. Workability The measured slump ﬂow of normal and lightweight concrete is plotted in Fig. 4 . The slump ﬂow of lightweight concrete without AE agent was slightly lower than that of normal concrete. Although coarse lightweight aggregates had a regular spherical shape, the ﬂow of the fresh mixture was hindered by high friction between ﬁne bottom ash particles with rough surfaces [13,17]. Moreover, the fresh lightweight concrete slumped less due to its lower self- weight [21]. When the AE agent was applied, entrained air was generated in the cement paste and the viscosity of concrete thereupon de- creased [3]. Moreover, the entrained air reduced the friction be- tween aggregate particles, hindering the ﬂow of fresh concrete mixture via a mechanism referred to as the air ball-bearing effect [3]. Therefore, as the addition of AE agent was increased from 0% to 1.0%, the slump ﬂow of lightweight concrete drastically in- creased from 200 mm to 570 mm. However, when the addition of AE agent was further increased from 1.0% to 1.5%, the slump ﬂow decreased to 485 mm, because the fresh lightweight concrete took on a whipped cream-like condition and was less ﬂowable than lightweight concrete with 1.0% of AE agent. In addition, segregation between the cement paste and light- weight aggregate did not occur, even when the slump ﬂow in- creased. The coarse expanded shale was light enough that it was not submerged in the concrete mixture. 3.2. Development of air bubbles Fig. 5 shows the lateral surface of 100 /C2 200 mm cylinder spec- imens of lightweight concrete. Note that the thick lines of lighter color on the specimens originate from printing the names of the specimens. When the AE agent was not used, large entrapped pores were irregularly distributed in the concrete, as shown in Fig. 5a. Further- more, the pore size became smaller and the distribution of pores was enhanced with the inclusion of 0.5% of AE agent, as shown in Fig. 5 b. With an increase of the addition of AE agent, the size of the pores increased and some abnormally large pores were generated, as shown in Fig. 5 c and d. It is noted that the coarse Table 4 Mix proportion. Specimen number Type of aggregate Addition of AE agent per cement (wt.%) w/c S/a Unit weight (kg/m 3) Fine agg. Coarse agg. Water Cement Fine agg. er porosity range, corresponding with lightweight concrete with AE agent ( Fig. 11 ). This indicates that the decrease of compressive strength was less sensitive when the pores were located in the lightweight aggregate, compared to the case where the pores form in the cement paste matrix. This phenomenon is attributed to the higher strength of mate- rials consisting of texture in lightweight aggregates relative to that of cement paste. It is noted that the texture means the solid net- work of porous structure in lightweight aggregates. The ﬁne bot- tom ash and coarse expanded shale were both treated with high temperature of more than 1150 /C176C, and as a result the materials consisting of texture in both bottom ash and expanded shale be- came ceramic with high density. As shown in Fig. 1 , the pore vol- umes of ﬁne bottom ash and coarse expanded shale with a radius between 1.7 nm and 300 nm showed lower values than those of quartz sand. Furthermore, the texture of the cement ma- trix was composed of hydration products with lower denseness. When the concrete was under certain loads, stress concentration occurred at texture around the pores [21,23]. The strength of the texture around the pores in lightweight aggregates against the Fig. 6.
